spec_match.rationale: Directly stages the spec's motivating asymmetry: parser-accepts-but-JS-rejects, with a round-trip rebuilder verifying the two paths now agree post-v143.

interactive.rationale: Five preset HTML snippets plus an editable textarea drive a live parser→rebuild round-trip with red/green parity verdict. Real DOM operations, not a static card.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Index promises 'parse an HTML snippet, then try to rebuild the same tree with createElement. Pre-v143 the parser accepted unicode tags but JS threw — v143 closes the gap.' — page delivers that with verdict.

edge_cases.rationale: Verdict normalises whitespace before comparison, which masks attribute-order differences (the page admits this). Never shows the inverse case (JS accepts something parser rejects — rare but exists for non-HTML namespaces), nor what happens when the input is a fragment with multiple roots.

references.rationale: See-also lists https://chromestatus.com/feature/6278918763708416 and the DOM spec.

design_system.rationale: All colours pull from CSS variables; ok/bad use --accent-emerald / --accent-rose with default --text-black foreground.

openQuestions.title: Replace whitespace-normalised string compare with structural diff
openQuestions.detail: The current parity check serialises both trees, collapses whitespace, then string-equal compares. That masks attribute order differences and any text-node fragmentation. A small structural diff (walk both trees in parallel, compare localName/attrs/childCount) would give a more honest verdict and would surface the rare cases where JS rebuilds slightly differently (e.g. CDATA in XML islands).

openQuestions.title: Cover the fragment / multi-root input case
openQuestions.detail: innerHTML happily parses `` but firstElementChild only grabs ``. Either reject multi-root input with a helpful message or extend the rebuild to walk every child of `wrapper.children`. Today the demo silently truncates.

openQuestions.title: Add the symmetric 'JS-only' edge
openQuestions.detail: There are a handful of names the relaxation makes JS accept but the HTML parser still won't produce from text (e.g. a tag starting with `<<` cannot be parsed). One row contrasting that direction would show the asymmetry is still not fully gone — JS is now strictly more permissive than the parser in a few corners.

summary: Clean motivating-asymmetry demo with five well-chosen presets. Biggest fix is replacing whitespace-normalised string compare with a structural diff so attribute-order and text-node differences stop hiding behind a false-positive 'success' verdict.
